He was condemned for an undisclosed term in the prison cells known as The Leads, in the attic of the Doge’s Palace. There, Casanova languished for more than a year, until he made a daring and miraculous escape through the roof. His escape made Casanova a minor celebrity in the courts of Europe, but it also provoked his long exile from Venice, lasting eighteen years. Now his career as a traveling adventurer began in earnest. It has been estimated that he covered nearly 40,000 miles in his lifetime, mostly by stagecoach on bumpy eighteenth century roads – which is a lot. Throughout his life, he would sooner or later appear in every single European city that counted, out of nowhere, and somehow introduce himself at the inner circles of the powers that may be. He always left an impression. He was the ultimate self-invented man. After his famous escape, he ended up in Paris in January 1757 (Childs 1989: 77). Casanova was here able to exploit his connections to the French Freemasons Game and Gambling and the Implosion of Liminality: Playing Modernity 163 (Casanova had previously joined the Freemasons in Lyon in 1750). He immediately started to mingle with the aristocracy and continued to ‘play the chameleon’, as he himself liked to describe it. He looked for a patron, and found one in Francois Joachim de Pierre de Bernis, a French noble and later Cardinal. De Bernis had been French ambassador in Venice where he had come to know Casanova (they had shared mistresses and joined each other in rather frivolous ‘supper parties’) and was now a powerful minister, about to become Minister for Foreign Affairs. De Bernis presented Casanova to Pâris-Duverney, who in 1751 had been responsible for the establishment of the famous École Militaire. He was now seeking means of raising funds for this military institution. In general, France was in dire need of income to sustain its military efforts in war-prone Europe. It is in this context that Casanova came up with the brilliant idea of introducing a state-run lotto. Another Italian, Jean Calzabigi, was also present as the idea was presented to Pâris-Duverney; Calzabigi would himself later introduce state- run lotteries in many other parts of Europe, including Brussels and Berlin (here personally charged by Frederick the Great). In Paris the lottery was acclaimed by decree on August 15, 1757, a watershed date in European history. The lottery operation involved the drawing of five numbers from a total of 90, a system that has been reproduced in many European countries since. 10 The enterprise enjoyed immediate success, and the drawing of the lots intensified from as soon as 1758. Casanova became one of the trustees of the first state lottery, and also one of its best ticket salesmen. It made him rich very quickly and further contributed to his immense popularity in the Parisian salons. As someone who allegedly knew about numbers, Casanova also gained the reputation of being an alchemist. Casanova had studied chemistry from an early age and it was also in this quality that he became extremely popular in Enlightenment Paris. De Bernis recognized Casanova’s endless qualities and suggested employing Casanova as a spy for the French state, a role that he accepted. His first task was a successful mission to the French naval forces at Dunkirk, assembled for a descent on England. 11 During the Seven Years’ War, Casanova was again called upon to increase the state revenues for financing the war efforts, and was entrusted the mission to sell French state bonds in Amsterdam – which he did, again very 10 Lotteries were not a completely novel invention, nor within France. The first private lottery with monetary prices was apparently in Florence in 1530, and soon thereafter Venice created the world’s first government monopoly of lotteries (Brenner and Brenner 1990: 9). The novelty in eighteenth century France lay with the fact that lotteries now became the exclusive domain of the state (private lotteries became outlawed), and that the budgets involved grew immensely in size, playing with big numbers and scientific calculations of how to maximize gain; the connections to the rise of probability theory are particularly tight. In short, lotteries became an integral element of the modern reason of state. 11 When Casanova returned to Venice in 1774, he took up this trade again as he became a spy for the Venetian inquisitors of state – the very same institution that had condemned him to jail two decades earlier. Liminality and the Modern 164 successfully. He was even offered a job at the ministry of finance, which he duly refused (a good bluffer also knows his limits). It is difficult to find more direct connections between modern state formation, warfare, the social action of trickster figures and the evolution of the element of play in mass-consuming leisure culture. Another of the games that was in fact brought from Venice to France in this period was roulette, called Biribi in Venice; this became a very popular game of chance during the violent 1790s in Paris. The roulette, the little wheel, then became the most popular game of chance in German territories throughout the nineteenth century, and then in all of Europe, including Russia (Dostoyevsky not only wrote about the roulette, but also played it, passionately). Since then, gambling has consistently gained ever more space. State-run lotteries have remained precious vehicles for raising ‘voluntary’ funds from citizens. Casinos now generate incomes that make the other branches of entertainment industries pale into insignificance. The more gambling is prohibited, the more it apparently thrives. The US today tops the list of Casino revenues. It is this gambling industry which is spreading to every corner of our lives: to bars, super markets, Ryanair flights and to the limitless hyperspace of on-line virtual gambling; for while it was surely the internet and neo-liberal deregulation that jointly provoked the exponential
